title: Choosing an Optimizer: A Decision Matrix
concept: A capstone comparison across SGD, Momentum, Nesterov, AdaGrad, RMSprop, Adam, and AdamW — weighing tradeoffs in speed, memory, and stability, and when each one is the sensible default choice.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what's the key difference between Adam and AdamW?

options.text: AdamW decouples weight decay into its own separate step instead of folding it into the adaptive gradient update
AdamW removes momentum from the optimizer entirely
AdamW no longer uses per-parameter learning rates

exercises.explain: Right — AdamW's decoupled weight decay is its one key refinement over Adam. You've now met seven optimizers across two units. Time to compare all of them side by side.

cards.title: Seven optimizers, one big question: which one?
cards.body: Across two units you've met: SGD → Momentum → Nesterov → AdaGrad → RMSprop → Adam → AdamW. Each one fixed a specific problem with the one before it. But in practice, when you sit down to train a real network, which one should you actually reach for?

cards.title: A decision matrix: speed, memory, stability
cards.body: Judge optimizers along three axes. Speed: how fast it converges to a good answer. Memory: how much extra bookkeeping it needs per parameter — plain SGD needs none, Momentum needs one running average, Adam and AdamW need two. Stability: how well it avoids zig-zagging, stalling on plateaus, or blowing up.

cards.title: A toolbox, not one perfect tool
cards.body: An experienced builder doesn't own a single wrench for every job — they carry a full toolbox and reach for whichever tool actually fits. Optimizers work the same way: a simple, well-tuned SGD might be exactly right for a small, well-behaved problem, while a huge modern network reaches for Adam or AdamW instead.

cards.title: The lineup, in one line each
cards.body: SGD: simplest baseline, zero memory overhead, needs careful tuning. Momentum: adds velocity to smooth zig-zagging. Nesterov: momentum with a look-ahead correction. AdaGrad: great for rare/sparse features, but its rate decays toward zero. RMSprop: fixes that decay with a moving average. Adam: momentum + RMSprop, a strong reliable default. AdamW: Adam with cleaner weight decay, the modern default for large models.

cards.title: There's no single "best" optimizer for every situation
cards.body: A common belief is that Adam or AdamW is always the objectively best choice, since it's the most popular default. In reality, a carefully-tuned SGD with Momentum sometimes generalizes better on certain tasks despite needing more tuning effort. The "best" choice always depends on the problem, the dataset, and how much tuning time is available.

left.text:
Minimal memory overhead
Needs careful learning-rate tuning
Can generalize very well when well-tuned

right.text:
Per-parameter rates, more memory
Converges fast with little tuning
AdamW is the modern large-model default

exercises.prompt: Which optimizer generally needs the LEAST extra memory per parameter?

options.text: Plain SGD, because it keeps no extra running averages at all

exercises.explain: Correct! Plain SGD needs zero extra bookkeeping per parameter, while Adam and AdamW each track two running averages per parameter.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Adam and AdamW are always the objectively best optimizer choice for every deep learning problem.
exercises.explain: False. The best choice depends on the problem, dataset, and tuning budget — a well-tuned SGD with Momentum sometimes generalizes better.

exercises.sentence: When judging optimizers, three key axes to weigh are speed, memory, and ___.
exercises.explain: Speed, memory, and stability are the three practical axes for comparing optimizers.

exercises.prompt: You're training a massive modern transformer model and want fast convergence, per-parameter adaptive rates, AND clean, decoupled regularization for the best generalization. Which optimizer is the standard modern default?
exercises.explain: AdamW is the standard modern default for exactly this combination of needs.

exercises.prompt: You need an optimizer for a huge modern deep network. It must converge reliably with minimal manual tuning, avoid AdaGrad's problem of learning rates decaying toward zero, and use clean regularization for the best generalization. Which optimizer satisfies ALL of these, and what earlier ideas does it combine?

options.text: AdamW — it combines Momentum's velocity smoothing and RMSprop's decaying adaptive rates (avoiding AdaGrad's permanent decay), plus decoupled weight decay for cleaner regularization
Plain SGD — because it's the simplest and requires no bookkeeping
AdaGrad — because it adapts a learning rate per parameter
Nesterov — because it looks ahead before computing its gradient

exercises.explain: Exactly right! You've now built the full optimizer toolkit: SGD's raw gradient steps, Momentum's velocity, Nesterov's look-ahead correction, AdaGrad's per-parameter rates, RMSprop's decay fix, Adam's fusion of momentum and adaptive rates, and AdamW's cleaner regularization. With this toolkit, you can defend a real optimizer choice for any training job — not just recite the names.
